title: Residuals: Measuring Individual Misses
concept: A residual is the vertical distance between a real data point and the model's prediction line.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the difference between slope and intercept?

options.text: Slope controls the steepness (angle) of the line, while intercept controls the starting height.
Slope controls starting height, while intercept controls the angle.
Slope is always positive, while intercept is always negative.

exercises.explain: Correct! Slope determines the tilt/rate of change, and intercept determines where the line crosses the y-axis (when x is 0).

cards.title: Measuring a miss
cards.body: If a model predicts a house is worth $300,000, but it actually sells for $320,000, the model missed by $20,000. In machine learning, we need to measure these individual misses for every single data point.

cards.title: What is a residual?
cards.body: A residual (or error) is the vertical distance between an actual data point and the prediction line. It is calculated as: Actual Value - Predicted Value.

cards.title: A ruler from point to line
cards.body: Imagine the line of best fit is a straight highway. The actual data points are houses scattered near the highway. A residual is like measuring a straight, vertical line from each house back to the highway.

cards.title: Calculating a residual
cards.body: If your line predicts a customer will spend $15 on lunch, but they spend $18, the residual is $3 (positive). If they only spend $11, the residual is -$4 (negative).

cards.title: Negatives don't mean zero error
cards.body: If you just add up all raw residuals, the positive errors (points above the line) and negative errors (points below) cancel each other out, making the sum close to zero. To fix this, ML models square the residuals so they all become positive!

cards.title: Positive vs. Negative Residual
cards.body: How residuals represent points relative to the line:

options.text: Predicted Value divided by Actual Value
The absolute sum of all features

exercises.explain: A residual is simply the difference: Actual - Predicted. It tells us how far off the prediction was.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A negative residual means the model's prediction was higher than the actual value.
exercises.explain: True. If actual is smaller than predicted, subtracting them results in a negative number, meaning the model overpredicted.

exercises.sentence: To prevent positive and negative misses from cancelling each other out, models often ___ the residuals.
exercises.explain: Squaring residuals turns all negative misses into positive values, preventing them from cancelling out.

exercises.prompt: If the actual price of a laptop is $1,200 and the model's line predicts it to be $1,000, what is the residual?
exercises.explain: Actual ($1,200) - Predicted ($1,000) = $200. This is a positive residual.

exercises.prompt: You train a regression model to predict commute time based on distance. For a 10-mile commute, the model predicts 20 minutes. If a driver takes 28 minutes, and another takes 15 minutes, what are their respective residuals?
exercises.explain: Brilliant! The first driver took longer than predicted, yielding a positive residual of 28 - 20 = +8. The second was faster, yielding a negative residual of 15 - 20 = -5. Now, let's explore the essential assumptions linear regression relies on to work properly!
